demo_narrative: Standard Intra-Asia run. Clear skies, calm South China Sea. No disruption signals. Vessel on optimal schedule. No intervention warranted.
expected_reason: Disruption=None, clear weather, low geo-risk (0.22). 0.8% base delay rate for no-disruption rows in dataset. XGBoost returns low cong_prob → RL state: Low congestion → Q-table selects Maintain Course.

demo_narrative: Shanghai–Busan feeder run. Moderate Yellow Sea swell (WSI 3.5/10) within seasonal norms — not a disruption signal. High-frequency NE Asia service, on-schedule departure. Congestion probability below threshold. No intervention warranted.
expected_reason: Short NE Asia feeder lane. Disruption=None. Geo-risk 0.28 well below RL BAND_LOW threshold (0.30). Yellow Sea swell (WSI 3.5/10) within seasonal norm — not a disruption event. XGBoost returns low cong_prob → Q-table selects Maintain Course.

demo_narrative: Singapore–Jebel Ali via Arabian Sea. Elevated Red Sea tension (geo_risk 0.52) with Jebel Ali berth congestion advisory (8–10hr queue) — not a full Hormuz closure scenario. Slow steaming from 18kt to 13kt times arrival after berth queue clears, saving $3,200 bunker fuel while maintaining route integrity.
expected_reason: Elevated Red Sea tension (geo_risk 0.52) + Jebel Ali berth queue advisory. Port Congestion disruption type → cong_prob in moderate band (0.35–0.50). NOT a full geopolitical crisis — RL correctly selects Slow Steam over Reroute. Slowing from 18kt to 13kt times Hormuz transit and berth arrival.

alternative_routes.name: Full Speed (Risk: Berth Queue + Hormuz Delay)
alternative_routes.description: Maintain 18kt, arrive during peak Jebel Ali congestion and elevated Hormuz traffic window.
alternative_routes.feasibility: LOW — 12hr berth wait projected with Hormuz transit delay risk

alternative_routes.name: Colombo Transhipment (if Hormuz escalates to crisis)
alternative_routes.description: Divert to Colombo for transhipment; short-sea onward to Jebel Ali avoiding Hormuz entirely. Only warranted if situation worsens.
alternative_routes.feasibility: MEDIUM — contingency only, not current threat level

demo_narrative: Mumbai–Jebel Ali feeder, Arabian Sea leg. Port congestion advisory at Jebel Ali with 8–10hr berth queue (Arabian Sea moderate swell WSI 4.2/10). Reducing from 16kt to 11kt saves $3,200 bunker fuel and synchronises arrival with berth clearance. No reroute warranted at current threat level.
expected_reason: Arabian Sea congestion (geo_risk 0.48) + Jebel Ali port delay advisory. Port Congestion disruption type → cong_prob in moderate band. Not severe enough to trigger reroute — no geopolitical threat active. Slow Steam times berth arrival and conserves fuel on short 1,650nm Gulf run.

alternative_routes.name: Full Speed (Risk: Berth Queue at Jebel Ali)
alternative_routes.description: Maintain 16kt, arrive during peak Jebel Ali congestion. Dataset: Port Congestion = 91.4% delay probability.

alternative_routes.name: Sharjah Port Diversion
alternative_routes.description: Divert to Khalid Port, Sharjah (18nm from Jebel Ali) if Jebel Ali congestion worsens beyond advisory level.
alternative_routes.feasibility: MEDIUM — capacity limited for large vessels

demo_narrative: REROUTE ALERT: Yantian→ECT Rotterdam. Active Houthi threat in Red Sea (WSI 6.8/10, geo_risk 0.78). War-risk insurance surcharge 0.5% cargo activated. Rerouting via Cape of Good Hope (+6,500nm, +14 transit days) avoids war zone and demurrage exposure. Net saving $12,400 per order after extra fuel costs.
expected_reason: Suez / Red Sea geopolitical conflict (geo_risk 0.78, disruption=geopolitical). Dataset: 100% delay rate for geopolitical events on Suez corridor. War-risk surcharge active (0.5% cargo value). Cape of Good Hope bypass avoids $18,900 demurrage + war-risk insurance, net saving $12,400 per order after extra fuel costs.

alternative_routes.name: Cape of Good Hope Bypass (Primary Reroute)
alternative_routes.description: Shenzhen → Singapore → Cape Town → Rotterdam. Avoids Red Sea / Suez entirely. +6,500nm, +14 transit days. War-risk insurance and Suez tolls eliminated.
alternative_routes.feasibility: HIGH — recommended reroute, fully clear of conflict zone

alternative_routes.name: Eurasian Land Bridge (Rail — High-Value Cargo)
alternative_routes.description: Shenzhen → Busan → Rail via Trans-Siberian → Rotterdam. Fastest alternative for time-critical electronics shipments.
alternative_routes.risk_avoided: All maritime risk on Suez corridor
alternative_routes.feasibility: MEDIUM — rail capacity constrained, premium cost

demo_narrative: Singapore–Sydney. Category 3 cyclone active in Coral Sea (WSI 7.4/10). Standard routing through Torres Strait directly into cyclone path. Rerouting via southern Indonesian archipelago (Banda Sea → Timor Sea south) adds 1,200nm but avoids cyclone entirely. Staggered 18hr hold also viable for fast-moving systems.
expected_reason: Cyclone advisory in Coral Sea / Australian approach (weather=Severe, WSI 7.4/10). Combined geo_risk 0.58 + severe_weather disruption → cong_prob in high band (≥0.55). RL weather floor: Severe weather overrides Maintain Course entirely. Southern diversion via Indonesia adds 1,200nm but clears the cyclone path entirely.

alternative_routes.name: Southern Indonesian Diversion (Recommended)
alternative_routes.description: Singapore → Banda Sea → Timor Sea South → Pacific approach → Sydney. Clears Coral Sea cyclone track entirely. +1,200nm.
alternative_routes.feasibility: HIGH — recommended reroute, full cyclone avoidance
alternative_routes.dp_world_terminal: N/A — direct call to Port Botany

alternative_routes.name: Delay + Staggered Routing (18hr Hold at Singapore)
alternative_routes.description: Hold at Singapore PSA for 18hr, then proceed on standard route once cyclone clears north of the track. Only viable if cyclone forecast confidence is high and system is fast-moving.
alternative_routes.risk_avoided: Cyclone path (partial — depends on track forecast)
alternative_routes.feasibility: MEDIUM — contingency only, subject to Met forecast update
